* **Gowers' Sign**: Using hands to 'walk up' legs to stand.
* **Pseudohypertrophy**: Large calves due to fat/fibrosis.
* **Precaution**: Avoid eccentric or high-intensity exercise (muscle damage).

title: Cystic Fibrosis (CF)
content:
### Definition
Genetic disorder causing thick, sticky mucus. Leads to frequent infections and obstructive lung disease.

### PT Role
Airway clearance (Percussion/Postural Drainage) and exercise tolerance.

title: Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo (BPPV)
content:
### Mechanism
Crystals (otoconia) displaced into semicircular canals. Most common in **posterior canal**.

### Test
**Dix-Hallpike Test**: Upbeating/torsional nystagmus indicates posterior canal BPPV.

title: Epley Maneuver
content:
### Purpose
Canalith repositioning maneuver for Posterior Canal BPPV. High success rate (90%+).

title: Meniere's Disease
content:
### Symptoms
Triad: Vertigo, Tinnitus, and Hearing Loss. Caused by excessive fluid (endolymph) in the inner ear.

title: Vestibular Neuritis
content:
### Definition
Inflammation of the vestibular nerve. Constant vertigo and nausea. **Hearing is spared** (unlike Labyrinthitis).

title: Parkinson's: Hoehn and Yahr Scale
content:
### Stages
* **Stage 1**: Unilateral symptoms.
* **Stage 2**: Bilateral symptoms, no balance impairment.
* **Stage 3**: Impaired balance, physically independent.
* **Stage 4**: Severe disability; can walk/stand with assistance.
* **Stage 5**: Wheelchair or bedbound.

title: CVA: Middle Cerebral Artery (MCA) Syndrome
content:
### Presentation
Most common stroke. Contralateral hemiparesis/sensory loss, **UE more affected than LE**. If left hemisphere, Aphasia is likely.

title: CVA: Anterior Cerebral Artery (ACA) Syndrome
content:
### Presentation
Contralateral hemiparesis/sensory loss, **LE more affected than UE**. Significant behavioral and personality changes.

title: Aphasia: Broca’s vs. Wernicke’s
content:
### Comparison
* **Broca’s (Expressive)**: Non-fluent, choppy speech; comprehension is intact.
* **Wernicke’s (Receptive)**: Fluent, 'word salad' speech; comprehension is impaired.

title: Goniometry: Shoulder External Rotation
content:
### Standards
* **Fulcrum**: Olecranon process.
* **Proximal Arm**: Perpendicular to floor.
* **Distal Arm**: Midline of ulna.
* **Normal Range**: 0-90 degrees.
references: Norkin and White, Measurement of Joint Motion

title: Lumber Mobilization: Anterior Glide
content:
### Purpose
To increase lumbar **extension**.

title: Types of Incontinence: Urge
content:
### Definition
Sudden, strong desire to void. Associated with 'key-in-the-lock' syndrome. Treated with bladder retraining and avoiding irritants (caffeine).
references: Goodman and Fuller, Pathology for PTs

title: Sinding-Larsen-Johansson Syndrome
content:
### Definition
Apophysitis of the **inferior pole of the patella**. Occurs in active adolescents. Differentiated from Osgood-Schlatter (tibial tubercle).

title: Tennis Elbow vs. Golfer’s Elbow
content:
### Comparison
* **Tennis (Lateral)**: Extensor Carpi Radialis Brevis (ECRB) origin.
* **Golfer’s (Medial)**: Pronator Teres and Flexor Carpi Radialis (FCR) origin.

title: Legg-Calve-Perthes Disease
content:
### Definition
Avascular necrosis of the femoral head in children (ages 4-8). Presents with limping and decreased hip abduction/internal rotation.

title: Slipped Capital Femoral Epiphysis (SCFE)
content:
### Definition
Femoral head slips posteriorly. Typically seen in **obese adolescents**. Leg sits in external rotation.

title: Scoliosis: Risser Sign
content:
### Definition
Grading of the ossification of the iliac apophysis to estimate skeletal maturity. Grade 0-5. Higher grade = less risk of curve progression.

title: Goniometry: Great Toe Extension
content:
### Standards
* **Fulcrum**: MTP joint.
* **Proximal Arm**: 1st metatarsal.
* **Distal Arm**: Proximal phalanx.
* **Normal Range**: 0-70 degrees (required for normal gait).
references: Norkin and White, Measurement of Joint Motion

title: Gait: Foot Slap
content:
### Presentation
Audible slap of the foot at loading response.

### Cause
Mild weakness of the dorsiflexors. Muscle cannot control the descent of the foot eccentrically.

title: Gait: Toe Drag
content:
### Cause
Severe weakness of dorsiflexors (Tibialis Anterior) or spasticity of plantarflexors during swing phase.

title: Sensitivity vs. Specificity
content:
### Mnemonic (SnNout / SpPIn)
* **Sensitivity**: High sensitivity rules OUT a condition (Negative result is reliable).
* **Specificity**: High specificity rules IN a condition (Positive result is reliable).
references: Portney and Watkins, Foundations of Clinical Research

title: Likelihood Ratios (LR)
content:
### Values
* **LR+ > 10**: Strong evidence to rule in.
* **LR- < 0.1**: Strong evidence to rule out.
* **LR = 1**: Test provides no useful information.
references: Portney and Watkins, Foundations of Clinical Research

title: Intervention: High-Voltage Pulsed Current (HVPC) for Edema
content:
### Parameters
Negative polarity used for acute edema (inflammation phase) to repel negatively charged plasma proteins.

title: Iontophoresis: Acetic Acid
content:
### Indication
Used for **calcific tendonitis** or myositis ossificans. Negatively charged.

title: Intervention: PNF Hold-Relax
content:
### Purpose
To increase ROM. Uses isometric contraction of the **antagonist** (tight) muscle followed by relaxation and passive stretch. (Autogenic inhibition).
references: Voss et al., Proprioceptive Neuromuscular Facilitation

title: Intervention: PNF Agonist Contract
content:
### Purpose
To increase ROM. Concentric contraction of the **agonist** (muscle opposite the tightness) to inhibit the tight muscle. (Reciprocal inhibition).
references: Voss et al., Proprioceptive Neuromuscular Facilitation

title: Intervention: Rhythmic Stabilization
content:
### Purpose
To improve **stability** and co-contraction. Applied using isometric contractions of agonists and antagonists in a rotatory pattern.
references: Voss et al., Proprioceptive Neuromuscular Facilitation

title: Cranial Nerve II (Optic) vs. III (Oculomotor) Reflex
content:
### Pupillary Light Reflex
* **CN II**: Sensory (Afferent) limb.
* **CN III**: Motor (Efferent) limb causing pupil constriction.

title: Cranial Nerve V (Trigeminal) vs. VII (Facial) Reflex
content:
### Corneal Reflex
* **CN V**: Sensory (Afferent) limb - feeling the touch on the eye.
* **CN VII**: Motor (Efferent) limb - blinking.

title: Cranial Nerve IX (Glossopharyngeal) vs. X (Vagus) Reflex
content:
### Gag Reflex
* **CN IX**: Sensory (Afferent) limb.
* **CN X**: Motor (Efferent) limb.

title: Guillain-Barre Syndrome (GBS)
content:
### Presentation
Acute, **ascending**, symmetrical paralysis (LMN). Often follows a viral infection. PT must avoid over-fatigue during the recovery phase.

title: Multiple Sclerosis (MS) Types
content:
### Categories
* **Relapsing-Remitting**: Most common; clear relapses with full/partial recovery.
* **Primary Progressive**: Steady decline from onset.
* **Secondary Progressive**: Starts as RRMS, then turns into steady decline.

title: MS: Uhthoff’s Phenomenon
content:
### Definition
Temporary worsening of neurological symptoms (e.g., blurred vision) due to **increased body temperature** (heat intolerance).

title: Pneumothorax Signs
content:
### Symptoms
Sudden onset chest pain, SOB, tracheal deviation **away** from the affected side (tension pneumothorax), and absent breath sounds.

title: Pulmonary Embolism (PE) Signs
content:
### Symptoms
Sudden SOB, chest pain, hemoptysis (blood in spit), and tachycardia. Use **Wells' Criteria** to assess risk.

title: Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) Signs
content:
### Symptoms
Unilateral leg swelling, warmth, and calf pain. **Homan's sign** is unreliable. Use Wells' Criteria.

title: Shoulder Mobilization: Lateral Distraction
content:
### Purpose
To improve **overall joint mobility** and decrease pain.

title: Goniometry: Ankle Plantarflexion
content:
### Standards
* **Fulcrum**: Lateral malleolus.
* **Proximal Arm**: Lateral midline of fibula.
* **Distal Arm**: Parallel to 5th metatarsal.
* **Normal Range**: 0-50 degrees.
references: Norkin and White, Measurement of Joint Motion

title: Goniometry: Ankle Inversion
content:
### Standards
* **Fulcrum**: Anterior midline of the ankle (between malleoli).
* **Proximal Arm**: Anterior midline of the tibia.
* **Distal Arm**: Dorsal midline of the 2nd metatarsal.
* **Normal Range**: 0-35 degrees.
references: Norkin and White, Measurement of Joint Motion

title: Intervention: Aquatic Therapy (Buoyancy)
content:
### Effect
Upward force that opposes gravity. Reduces weight-bearing. Submersion to C7 neck reduces weight-bearing to **10%** of body weight.

title: Intervention: Aquatic Therapy (Hydrostatic Pressure)
content:
### Effect
Pressure exerted by water on submerged body. Increases with depth. Helps reduce peripheral edema and increases venous return.

title: CVA: Posterior Cerebral Artery (PCA) Syndrome
content:
### Presentation
Visual deficits (Homonymous Hemianopsia), memory loss, and **Thalamic Pain Syndrome**.

title: Homonymous Hemianopsia
content:
### Definition
Loss of the same half of the visual field in both eyes (e.g., losing the left side of vision in both eyes after a right CVA).

title: Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS)
content:
### Categories
* **Eye Opening** (1-4)
* **Verbal Response** (1-5)
* **Motor Response** (1-6)
### Scoring
* **< 8**: Severe TBI ('GCS of 8, intubate').
* **9-12**: Moderate TBI.
* **13-15**: Mild TBI.

title: Ranchos Los Amigos: Level I
content:
### Definition
**No Response**: Patient is in a deep sleep and does not respond to any stimuli.

title: Ranchos Los Amigos: Level IV
content:
### Definition
**Confused-Agitated**: Heightened state of activity. Detached from present; aggressive behavior. Short attention span.

title: Ranchos Los Amigos: Level VII
content:
### Definition
**Automatic-Appropriate**: Robot-like daily routine. Minimal to no confusion; impaired judgment and foresight.

title: Gait: Foot Drop
content:
### Cause
Total loss of dorsiflexion strength. Foot remains in plantarflexion throughout swing phase.

title: Gait: Knee Hyperextension (Genu Recurvatum)
content:
### Causes
Weak quadriceps, spasticity of plantarflexors (extensor thrust), or compensation for weak hip extensors.

title: Gait: Lateral Trunk Lean
content:
### Cause
Weakness of hip abductors (Trendelenburg compensation) or hip pain.

title: Goniometry: Shoulder Flexion
content:
### Standards
* **Fulcrum**: Lateral aspect of the humerus (greater tubercle).
* **Proximal Arm**: Midaxillary line of thorax.
* **Distal Arm**: Lateral midline of humerus (lateral epicondyle).
* **Normal Range**: 0-180 degrees.
references: Norkin and White, Measurement of Joint Motion

title: Goniometry: Elbow Flexion
content:
### Standards
* **Fulcrum**: Lateral epicondyle of the humerus.
* **Proximal Arm**: Lateral midline of the humerus.
* **Distal Arm**: Lateral midline of the radius.
* **Normal Range**: 0-150 degrees.
references: Norkin and White, Measurement of Joint Motion

title: Goniometry: Forearm Pronation
content:
### Standards
* **Fulcrum**: Lateral styloid process of radius.
* **Proximal Arm**: Parallel to anterior midline of humerus.
* **Distal Arm**: Dorsal surface of forearm.
* **Normal Range**: 0-80 degrees.
references: Norkin and White, Measurement of Joint Motion

title: Tinel’s Sign (Median Nerve)
content:
### Procedure
Tap the median nerve at the wrist (carpal tunnel). Positive if paresthesia into thumb, index, middle finger.

title: Cervical Traction: Herniated Disc
content:
### Setting
Intermittent traction (60s on, 20s off) is often preferred for disc issues to allow for relief and re-lubrication.

title: Lumbar Traction: Disc Herniation
content:
### Force
Initial force of 30-45 lbs. Progress to 50% of body weight for effective vertebral separation.

title: Intervention: E-Stim for Denervated Muscle
content:
### Parameters
Requires **Direct Current (DC)** or very long pulse durations (> 10ms) because the nerve is no longer there to trigger the contraction.

title: Wrist Drop
content:
### Cause
Injury to the **Radial Nerve** (e.g., Saturday Night Palsy or mid-shaft humerus fracture). Loss of wrist and finger extensors.
references: Goodman and Fuller, Pathology for PTs

title: Ape Hand Deformity
content:
### Cause
Injury to the **Median Nerve**. Atrophy of the thenar eminence; thumb sits in plane with fingers.

title: Claw Hand Deformity
content:
### Cause
Injury to the **Ulnar Nerve**. Hyperextension of MCP and flexion of IP joints in the 4th/5th digits.

title: Intervention: PNF D1 Extension (UE)
content:
### Pattern
'Open your hand and push down and away.' Shoulder: Extension, Abduction, Internal Rotation. Forearm: Pronation.
references: Voss et al., Proprioceptive Neuromuscular Facilitation

title: Intervention: PNF D2 Extension (UE)
content:
### Pattern
'Close your hand and pull down and across.' Shoulder: Extension, Adduction, Internal Rotation. Forearm: Pronation.
references: Voss et al., Proprioceptive Neuromuscular Facilitation

title: Goniometry: Hip Abduction
content:
### Standards
* **Fulcrum**: ASIS of the limb being measured.
* **Proximal Arm**: Imaginary line between both ASIS.
* **Distal Arm**: Anterior midline of the femur (patella).
* **Normal Range**: 0-45 degrees.
references: Norkin and White, Measurement of Joint Motion

title: Goniometry: Hip External Rotation
content:
### Standards
* **Fulcrum**: Patella.
* **Proximal Arm**: Perpendicular to floor.
* **Distal Arm**: Midline of tibia.
* **Normal Range**: 0-45 degrees.
references: Norkin and White, Measurement of Joint Motion

title: Intervention: Mechanical Lift (Hoyer)
content:
### Safety
Always requires 2 people for transfer. Used for patients with Total Assist (FIM 1) or who exceed the safe lifting limit of the clinician.

title: Wheelchair: Standard Seat Width
content:
### Measurement
Measure widest part of hips and **add 2 inches**. Standard adult size is 18 inches.

title: Wheelchair: Standard Seat Depth
content:
### Measurement
Measure from posterior buttock to popliteal fold and **subtract 2 inches**. Standard adult size is 16 inches.

title: Anterior Cruciate Ligament (ACL) Rehabilitation
content:
### Introduction
The ACL is a vital stabilizer of the knee. Post-operative rehab follows a structured phase-based approach.

### Phases of Rehab
*   **Phase I**: Protection and Range of Motion (Week 1-4)
*   **Phase II**: Strengthening and Functional Tasks (Week 5-12)
*   **Phase III**: Return to Sport (Month 4+)

title: Rotator Cuff Tendinopathy Management
content:
### Overview
Rotator cuff tendinopathy involves degenerative changes in the shoulder tendons, commonly affecting supraspinatus.

### Treatment Approach
*   **Pain Management**: Modalities and activity modification
*   **Progressive Loading**: Eccentric exercises and scapular stabilization
*   **Functional Training**: Sport-specific or ADL-related movements

title: Stroke Rehabilitation: Acute Phase
content:
### Immediate Post-Stroke Care
Early mobilization within 24-48 hours improves outcomes and reduces complications.

### Key Interventions
*   **Positioning**: Prevent contractures and pressure ulcers
*   **Sitting Balance**: Progressive challenges to trunk control
*   **Transfer Training**: Bed mobility and sit-to-stand

title: Parkinson's Disease: Gait Training
content:
### Gait Characteristics
Patients with PD exhibit shuffling gait, reduced arm swing, and freezing episodes.

### Intervention Strategies
*   **Cueing**: Visual, auditory, and tactile cues to improve step length
*   **Dual-Task Training**: Cognitive-motor interference management
*   **LSVT BIG**: Amplitude-focused movement therapy

title: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD) Management
content:
### Disease Overview
COPD involves progressive airflow limitation and breathing difficulty.

### PT Interventions
*   **Breathing Techniques**: Pursed-lip and diaphragmatic breathing
*   **Exercise Training**: Endurance and strength programs
*   **Energy Conservation**: Pacing and activity modification

title: Total Hip Arthroplasty: Posterior Approach Precautions
content:
### Post-Operative Precautions
Posterior approach THA requires specific movement restrictions to prevent dislocation.

### Hip Precautions (6-12 weeks)
*   **Avoid**: Hip flexion >90°, adduction past midline, internal rotation
*   **Safe Transfers**: Use elevated seats and assistive devices
*   **Progressive Weight-Bearing**: As per surgeon protocol

title: Spinal Cord Injury: C6 Functional Expectations
content:
### Muscle Innervation at C6
Patients retain shoulder movement, elbow flexion, wrist extension, but lack triceps and hand intrinsics.

### Functional Capabilities
*   **Transfers**: Independent with equipment
*   **Wheelchair Propulsion**: Manual wheelchair on level surfaces
*   **ADLs**: Modified independence with adaptive devices

title: Plantar Fasciitis Treatment
content:
### Pathophysiology
Overuse injury causing heel pain, worst with first steps in morning.

### Evidence-Based Interventions
*   **Stretching**: Calf and plantar fascia stretches
*   **Orthotics**: Arch support and heel cushioning
*   **Manual Therapy**: Soft tissue mobilization
*   **Progressive Loading**: Gradual return to activity

title: Cardiac Rehabilitation Phase II
content:
### Outpatient Cardiac Rehab
Phase II begins 2-3 weeks post-event or post-surgery, typically lasting 12 weeks.

### Components
*   **Exercise Training**: 3-5 days/week, 60-80% HRmax
*   **Monitoring**: ECG, BP, symptoms, RPE
*   **Education**: Risk factor modification and lifestyle changes

title: Lymphedema Management: Complete Decongestive Therapy
content:
### CDT Components
Gold standard treatment for lymphedema involves four main elements.

### Treatment Elements
*   **Manual Lymph Drainage**: Specialized massage technique
*   **Compression Bandaging**: Multi-layer short-stretch bandages
*   **Exercise**: Gentle movement with compression
*   **Skin Care**: Hygiene and infection prevention

title: Vestibular Rehabilitation: BPPV Canalith Repositioning
content:
### Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo
Most common cause of vertigo; treated with repositioning maneuvers.

### Epley Maneuver (Posterior Canal)
*   **Step 1**: Head turned 45° toward affected side
*   **Step 2**: Recline patient to supine with head extended
*   **Step 3**: Rotate head 90° to opposite side
*   **Step 4**: Roll patient onto side, sit up

title: Diabetic Foot Ulcer Prevention
content:
### Risk Factors
Peripheral neuropathy and vascular insufficiency increase ulceration risk.

### Prevention Strategies
*   **Foot Inspection**: Daily self-examination
*   **Proper Footwear**: Well-fitted shoes with adequate toe box
*   **Monofilament Testing**: Screen for sensory loss
*   **Patient Education**: Nail care and injury prevention

title: Pelvic Floor Dysfunction: Stress Urinary Incontinence
content:
### Pathophysiology
Weakness of pelvic floor muscles leading to urine leakage with increased intra-abdominal pressure.

### Treatment Approach
*   **Kegel Exercises**: Isolated pelvic floor contractions
*   **Biofeedback**: EMG or pressure sensors for motor learning
*   **Functional Training**: Integrate with daily activities

title: Adhesive Capsulitis (Frozen Shoulder) Management
content:
### Clinical Stages
Progresses through freezing, frozen, and thawing phases over 12-36 months.

### Stage-Specific Treatment
*   **Freezing**: Pain management, gentle ROM
*   **Frozen**: Aggressive stretching and joint mobilization
*   **Thawing**: Progressive strengthening and functional activities

title: Multiple Sclerosis: Fatigue Management
content:
### MS-Related Fatigue
Primary symptom affecting 75-95% of patients; distinct from general tiredness.

### Management Strategies
*   **Energy Conservation**: Prioritize activities and rest breaks
*   **Cooling**: Prevent heat-related symptom exacerbation
*   **Aerobic Exercise**: Moderate intensity improves fatigue
*   **Sleep Hygiene**: Address sleep disturbances

title: Guillain-Barré Syndrome Rehabilitation
content:
### Acute Inflammatory Demyelinating Polyneuropathy
Rapid-onset ascending paralysis requiring intensive rehab.

### Rehabilitation Phases
*   **Acute**: Positioning, ROM, respiratory management
*   **Plateau**: Gentle strengthening, avoid overwork
*   **Recovery**: Progressive resistance training and functional mobility

title: Lumbar Spinal Stenosis Conservative Management
content:
### Clinical Presentation
Neurogenic claudication with pain relief in flexed positions.

### Treatment Approach
*   **Flexion-Based Exercises**: Reduce symptoms by increasing canal space
*   **Core Stabilization**: Improve spinal support
*   **Aerobic Conditioning**: Stationary cycling preferred
*   **Epidural Injections**: When conservative care insufficient

title: Cerebral Palsy: Spastic Diplegia
content:
### Characteristics
Bilateral lower extremity spasticity with relatively spared upper extremities.

### Intervention Focus
*   **Tone Management**: Stretching, serial casting, botulinum toxin
*   **Gait Training**: Assistive devices and orthoses
*   **Functional Mobility**: Age-appropriate motor skills
*   **Family Education**: Home program and positioning

title: Wound Healing Phases
content:
### Three Overlapping Phases
Understanding wound healing guides intervention timing.

### Phase Characteristics
*   **Inflammatory (0-4 days)**: Hemostasis and cleaning
*   **Proliferative (4-21 days)**: Granulation and epithelialization
*   **Remodeling (21 days-2 years)**: Collagen reorganization and scar maturation

title: Achilles Tendon Repair Rehabilitation
content:
### Post-Surgical Protocol
Progressive loading following surgical repair of Achilles rupture.

### Rehabilitation Timeline
*   **Phase I (0-6 weeks)**: Protected weight-bearing in boot
*   **Phase II (6-12 weeks)**: Progressive ROM and strengthening
*   **Phase III (12+ weeks)**: Plyometrics and sport-specific training
*   **Return to Sport**: Typically 6-9 months

title: Bronchopulmonary Dysplasia in Infants
content:
### Chronic Lung Disease of Prematurity
Occurs in premature infants requiring prolonged mechanical ventilation.

### PT Management
*   **Positioning**: Optimize ventilation-perfusion matching
*   **Airway Clearance**: Gentle techniques as tolerated
*   **Developmental Activities**: Support motor milestones
*   **Family Training**: Home management strategies

title: Temporomandibular Joint Dysfunction
content:
### TMJ Disorders
Pain and dysfunction in jaw joint and muscles of mastication.

### Conservative Treatment
*   **Patient Education**: Avoid hard foods, control parafunctional habits
*   **Manual Therapy**: Joint mobilization and soft tissue techniques
*   **Exercises**: Postural correction and jaw ROM
*   **Modalities**: Heat, ice, ultrasound for symptom relief

title: Burn Classification and Acute Management
content:
### Burn Depth Classification
Determines healing time and need for surgical intervention.

### Depths and Management
*   **Superficial**: Epidermis only, heals in 3-7 days
*   **Superficial Partial-Thickness**: Papillary dermis, 7-21 days
*   **Deep Partial-Thickness**: Reticular dermis, may need grafting
*   **Full-Thickness**: All skin layers destroyed, requires grafting

title: Chronic Ankle Instability Rehabilitation
content:
### Recurrent Ankle Sprains
Repeated inversion injuries leading to mechanical and functional instability.

### Comprehensive Program
*   **Strengthening**: Peroneal and intrinsic foot muscles
*   **Proprioception**: Balance training on unstable surfaces
*   **Functional Activities**: Sport-specific drills
*   **Bracing/Taping**: External support during activities

title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) Management
content:
### Progressive Motor Neuron Disease
Requires adaptive approach as function declines.

### PT Role Throughout Disease
*   **Early Stage**: Maintain strength without overwork fatigue
*   **Middle Stage**: Conserve energy, assistive devices
*   **Late Stage**: Positioning, caregiver training, comfort
*   **Throughout**: Equipment recommendations and home modifications

title: Pregnancy-Related Low Back Pain
content:
### Musculoskeletal Changes in Pregnancy
Hormonal and biomechanical factors contribute to pain.

### Safe Interventions
*   **Exercise**: Strengthening core and pelvic floor
*   **Postural Education**: Body mechanics for daily activities
*   **Manual Therapy**: Gentle mobilization and soft tissue work
*   **Support Garments**: Maternity belts for symptom relief

title: Pneumonia: Airway Clearance Techniques
content:
### Respiratory Infection Management
Assist with secretion mobilization and prevent complications.

### Techniques
*   **Positioning**: Postural drainage positions
*   **Percussion and Vibration**: Mechanical loosening of secretions
*   **Directed Cough**: Effective expectoration
*   **Incentive Spirometry**: Prevent atelectasis

title: Pressure Injury Prevention
content:
### Risk Assessment
Identify high-risk patients using Braden Scale or Norton Scale.

### Prevention Strategies
*   **Repositioning**: Turn q2h, use 30° lateral positioning
*   **Support Surfaces**: Pressure-redistributing mattresses
*   **Skin Care**: Keep clean and dry, moisturize
*   **Nutrition**: Adequate protein and calories for tissue health

title: Meniscus Tear: Conservative vs. Surgical Management
content:
### Decision-Making Factors
Location, pattern, patient age, and activity level influence treatment.

### Conservative Management
*   **Indications**: Degenerative tears, stable knees, older adults
*   **Treatment**: Strengthening, ROM, activity modification
*   **Outcomes**: Often comparable to surgery for degenerative tears

### Surgical Indications
Traumatic tears, mechanical symptoms, young active patients

title: Traumatic Brain Injury: Rancho Los Amigos Scale
content:
### Cognitive Recovery Levels
Eight levels describe emergence from coma and cognitive recovery.

### Key Levels
*   **Level I-III**: No response to localized response
*   **Level IV-VI**: Confused and agitated to confused-appropriate
*   **Level VII-VIII**: Automatic-appropriate to purposeful-appropriate

### Treatment varies by level, emphasizing environmental structure and cognitive support

title: Osteoporosis: Exercise Prescription
content:
### Bone Health Through Exercise
Weight-bearing and resistance exercises stimulate bone formation.

### Exercise Guidelines
*   **Weight-Bearing**: Walking, dancing, stair climbing
*   **Resistance Training**: 2-3 days/week, all major muscle groups
*   **Balance Training**: Fall prevention
*   **Avoid**: Spinal flexion exercises in severe osteoporosis

title: Peripheral Arterial Disease: Exercise Therapy
content:
### Claudication Management
Structured exercise improves walking distance and quality of life.

### Exercise Protocol
*   **Frequency**: 3-5 days per week
*   **Intensity**: Walk to moderate claudication pain
*   **Duration**: Rest when pain reaches 3-4/10, resume when subsides
*   **Progression**: Gradually increase duration to 30-50 minutes

title: Carpal Tunnel Syndrome: Conservative Treatment
content:
### Median Nerve Compression
Night symptoms and thenar weakness are hallmark signs.

### Non-Surgical Management
*   **Splinting**: Neutral wrist position, especially at night
*   **Nerve Gliding**: Median nerve mobilization exercises
*   **Ergonomic Modifications**: Workstation adjustments
*   **Activity Modification**: Reduce repetitive wrist motions

title: Down Syndrome: Physical Therapy Considerations
content:
### Characteristic Features
Hypotonia, ligamentous laxity, and developmental delays.

### PT Focus Areas
*   **Atlantoaxial Instability**: Screen before contact sports
*   **Gross Motor Development**: Address delayed milestones
*   **Strengthening**: Combat low muscle tone
*   **Cardiovascular**: Monitor for congenital heart defects

title: Complex Regional Pain Syndrome (CRPS) Management
content:
### Type I (formerly RSD)
Disproportionate pain following injury without confirmed nerve lesion.

### Interdisciplinary Treatment
*   **Desensitization**: Gradual sensory re-education
*   **Stress Loading**: Graded weight-bearing activities
*   **Mirror Therapy**: Visual feedback for pain reduction
*   **Graded Motor Imagery**: Cortical reorganization

title: Cystic Fibrosis: Airway Clearance
content:
### Genetic Disorder Affecting Lungs
Thick mucus requires regular clearance to prevent infections.

### Clearance Techniques
*   **High-Frequency Chest Wall Oscillation**: Vest therapy
*   **Positive Expiratory Pressure (PEP)**: Maintains airway patency
*   **Autogenic Drainage**: Controlled breathing technique
*   **Exercise**: Natural airway clearance mechanism

title: Patellofemoral Pain Syndrome Treatment
content:
### Anterior Knee Pain
Multifactorial condition common in active individuals.

### Evidence-Based Interventions
*   **Hip Strengthening**: Gluteus medius and hip external rotators
*   **Quadriceps Training**: VMO emphasis questionable, general quad strength important
*   **Foot Orthotics**: May help in excessive pronation
*   **Activity Modification**: Avoid aggravating positions

title: Bell's Palsy Rehabilitation
content:
### Acute Unilateral Facial Nerve Paralysis
Idiopathic condition with generally good prognosis.

### Rehabilitation Approach
*   **Eye Protection**: Artificial tears and taping if incomplete closure
*   **Gentle Exercises**: Avoid aggressive stretching
*   **Neuromuscular Re-education**: Mirror feedback and specific movements
*   **Electrical Stimulation**: Controversial, may cause synkinesis

title: Venous Insufficiency and Venous Ulcers
content:
### Chronic Venous Disease
Valvular incompetence leads to venous hypertension and ulceration.

### Management Strategy
*   **Compression Therapy**: 30-40 mmHg graduated compression
*   **Elevation**: Legs above heart level several times daily
*   **Exercise**: Calf muscle pump activation
*   **Wound Care**: Moist wound healing environment

title: Total Knee Arthroplasty Rehabilitation
content:
### Post-Operative Goals
Achieve functional ROM, strength, and independence in ADLs.

### Rehabilitation Phases
*   **Acute (0-2 weeks)**: ROM, quad sets, straight leg raises
*   **Subacute (2-6 weeks)**: Progress strengthening, normalize gait
*   **Advanced (6-12 weeks)**: Functional activities, return to desired activities
*   **Goals**: 0° extension, 110-120° flexion minimum

title: Rheumatoid Arthritis: Joint Protection Principles
content:
### Autoimmune Inflammatory Arthritis
Symmetric joint involvement requiring activity modification.

### Joint Protection Strategies
*   **Respect Pain**: Stop activities that cause pain
*   **Use Larger Joints**: Distribute forces across stronger joints
*   **Avoid Positions of Deformity**: Prevent ulnar drift, swan neck
*   **Balance Rest and Activity**: Prevent fatigue while maintaining function

title: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy Management
content:
### Progressive X-Linked Disorder
Pseudohypertrophy of calves and progressive weakness.

### PT Management Across Stages
*   **Ambulatory Phase**: Stretching, submaximal strengthening
*   **Early Non-Ambulatory**: Power wheelchair, positioning
*   **Late Stage**: Respiratory care, comfort positioning
*   **Throughout**: Prevent contractures, especially heel cords

title: Mechanical Ventilation: Weaning Process
content:
### Liberation from Ventilator
PT assists in optimizing weaning readiness.

### PT Role in Weaning
*   **Mobility**: Early mobilization improves outcomes
*   **Positioning**: Optimize ventilation-perfusion
*   **Secretion Management**: Airway clearance
*   **Inspiratory Muscle Training**: Controversial but may help in difficult weans

title: Lateral Epicondylitis (Tennis Elbow)
content:
### Extensor Tendinopathy
Overuse injury affecting wrist extensor origin at lateral epicondyle.

### Treatment Progression
*   **Initial**: Activity modification, counterforce bracing
*   **Eccentric Exercise**: Wrist extensor eccentric strengthening
*   **Manual Therapy**: Deep friction massage, mobilization
*   **Progressive Loading**: Gradual return to activities

title: Huntington's Disease: Movement Disorder Management
content:
### Genetic Neurodegenerative Disease
Chorea, cognitive decline, and psychiatric symptoms.

### PT Interventions
*   **Safety**: Fall prevention strategies
*   **Gait Training**: Compensate for involuntary movements
*   **Functional Mobility**: Maintain independence as long as possible
*   **Caregiver Education**: Transfer training and safety

title: Asthma: Exercise-Induced Bronchoconstriction
content:
### Exercise-Triggered Airway Narrowing
Occurs during or after exercise, especially in cold, dry air.

### Management Strategies
*   **Warm-Up**: Gradual 10-15 minute warm-up
*   **Environment**: Avoid cold, dry air when possible
*   **Medications**: Bronchodilator 15 minutes before exercise
*   **Breathing Techniques**: Nasal breathing to warm/humidify air

title: De Quervain's Tenosynovitis
content:
### First Dorsal Compartment Inflammation
Affects abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis.

### Clinical Features
*   **Finkelstein Test**: Positive when painful
*   **Treatment**: Thumb spica splint, activity modification
*   **Manual Therapy**: Soft tissue mobilization
*   **Corticosteroid Injection**: If conservative treatment fails

title: Scoliosis: Adolescent Idiopathic
content:
### Lateral Spinal Curvature
Most common type, affecting girls more than boys.

### Management Based on Curve Magnitude
*   **<20°**: Observation
*   **20-40°**: Consider bracing (Risser 0-2)
*   **>40-50°**: Surgical consideration
*   **PT Role**: Posture education, core strengthening, monitoring

title: Chronic Kidney Disease: Exercise Prescription
content:
### Renal Dysfunction and Exercise
Exercise improves cardiovascular health and quality of life.

### Exercise Guidelines
*   **Aerobic**: 3-5 days/week, moderate intensity
*   **Resistance**: 2-3 days/week, avoid during dialysis
*   **Precautions**: Monitor for hypotension, avoid vascular access arm
*   **Timing**: Not during or immediately after dialysis

title: Autistic Spectrum Disorder: Motor Skills Development
content:
### Motor Challenges in ASD
Many children with ASD have motor planning and coordination difficulties.

### PT Interventions
*   **Sensory Integration**: Address sensory processing issues
*   **Gross Motor Skills**: Improve coordination and body awareness
*   **Social Play**: Incorporate motor skills in play activities
*   **Family Collaboration**: Consistent approaches across settings

title: Congestive Heart Failure: Exercise Training
content:
### Exercise Benefits in Stable CHF
Improves functional capacity and quality of life without increasing risk.

### Exercise Prescription
*   **Aerobic**: 3-5 days/week, 40-80% HRR
*   **Resistance**: Light weights, higher repetitions
*   **Contraindications**: Unstable symptoms, recent decompensation
*   **Monitoring**: Daily weights, dyspnea scale, vital signs

title: Osgood-Schlatter Disease
content:
### Tibial Tubercle Apophysitis
Common in adolescent athletes during growth spurts.

### Management Approach
*   **Activity Modification**: Reduce high-impact activities
*   **Stretching**: Quadriceps and hamstring flexibility
*   **Strengthening**: Eccentric quadriceps exercises
*   **Ice**: After activities for symptom control
*   **Prognosis**: Self-limiting, resolves with skeletal maturity

title: Urinary Incontinence: Urge Type
content:
### Overactive Bladder
Sudden, intense urge to urinate with possible leakage.

### Behavioral Interventions
*   **Bladder Training**: Scheduled voiding and urge suppression
*   **Pelvic Floor Exercises**: Strengthen urethral closure
*   **Fluid Management**: Appropriate timing and volume
*   **Bladder Irritant Avoidance**: Caffeine, alcohol, carbonation

title: Pes Planus (Flat Feet) Management
content:
### Reduced Medial Longitudinal Arch
Can be flexible (normal in children) or rigid (pathological).

### Treatment for Symptomatic Flat Feet
*   **Orthotics**: Arch support for rigid flat feet or symptoms
*   **Strengthening**: Intrinsic foot muscles, posterior tibialis
*   **Stretching**: Gastrocnemius and soleus
*   **Activity Modification**: Reduce high-impact activities during acute pain

title: Sepsis: Early Mobility in ICU
content:
### Critical Illness and Immobility
Early mobilization reduces delirium and improves outcomes.

### Progressive Mobility Protocol
*   **Level 1**: Passive ROM, positioning
*   **Level 2**: Active ROM, sitting edge of bed
*   **Level 3**: Sitting in chair, standing
*   **Level 4**: Ambulation
*   **Safety**: Hemodynamic stability, appropriate staffing

title: Interstitial Lung Disease: Exercise Prescription
content:
### Restrictive Lung Disease
Pulmonary fibrosis leads to reduced lung compliance and gas exchange.

### Exercise Modifications
*   **Oxygen Supplementation**: Maintain SpO2 >88-90%
*   **Interval Training**: Allow for recovery between bouts
*   **Lower Extremity Focus**: Reduce ventilatory demand
*   **Energy Conservation**: Pace activities, use assistive devices

title: Hip Labral Tear Management
content:
### Acetabular Labrum Injury
Causes deep groin pain with clicking or catching.

### Conservative Treatment
*   **Activity Modification**: Avoid provocative positions (flexion + IR)
*   **Strengthening**: Hip stabilizers, especially gluteus medius
*   **Manual Therapy**: Joint mobilization for ROM
*   **Core Stabilization**: Lumbar-pelvic control
*   **Surgical Consideration**: If conservative fails after 3-6 months

title: Erb's Palsy (Brachial Plexus Birth Injury)
content:
### Upper Trunk Injury (C5-C6)
Results in "waiter's tip" position of affected arm.

### Management Approach
*   **Early Intervention**: ROM to prevent contractures
*   **Positioning**: Avoid prolonged adduction/internal rotation
*   **Functional Activities**: Encourage bilateral hand use
*   **Family Education**: Home program and handling
*   **Surgical Consideration**: If no recovery by 3-6 months

title: Gout: Acute Attack Management
content:
### Crystal-Induced Arthropathy
Uric acid crystals deposit in joints, causing severe inflammation.

### PT Role During Acute Phase
*   **Rest**: Affected joint during acute flare
*   **Ice**: May provide symptomatic relief
*   **Positioning**: Elevate affected limb
*   **Education**: Dietary modifications, hydration
*   **Rehabilitation**: Begin after inflammation subsides

title: Postural Orthostatic Tachycardia Syndrome (POTS)
content:
### Autonomic Dysfunction
Excessive heart rate increase upon standing without hypotension.

### Exercise-Based Treatment
*   **Recumbent Exercise**: Start with rowing or recumbent bike
*   **Gradual Progression**: Slowly increase upright activity tolerance
*   **Fluid/Salt Loading**: Increase blood volume
*   **Compression Garments**: Abdominal and lower extremity
*   **Deconditioning Prevention**: Key component of management

title: Sacroiliac Joint Dysfunction
content:
### SI Joint Pain
Controversial diagnosis with varied presentation.

### Assessment and Treatment
*   **Provocative Tests**: Cluster of tests increases diagnostic accuracy
*   **Mobilization**: Grade III-IV for hypomobility
*   **Stabilization**: Core and hip strengthening for hypermobility
*   **Sacroiliac Belt**: May provide temporary relief
*   **Activity Modification**: Avoid asymmetrical loading

title: Breast Cancer: Post-Mastectomy Rehabilitation
content:
### Post-Surgical Complications
Lymphedema, ROM limitations, and pain common after surgery.

### Rehabilitation Components
*   **ROM Exercises**: Begin day 1 post-op per protocol
*   **Scar Mobilization**: Once incision healed
*   **Lymphedema Prevention**: Education and early intervention
*   **Strengthening**: Gradual progression, no weight restrictions with evidence
*   **Psychosocial Support**: Address body image and emotional needs

title: Spina Bifida: Myelomeningocele Management
content:
### Neural Tube Defect
Level of lesion determines motor and sensory function.

### PT Management by Level
*   **Thoracic**: Wheelchair primary mobility, standing frame
*   **High Lumbar (L1-L2)**: Wheelchair primary, therapeutic ambulation
*   **Low Lumbar (L3-L4)**: Community ambulation with AFOs
*   **Sacral**: Functional ambulation, minimal assistive devices
*   **All Levels**: Prevent pressure ulcers, monitor for hydrocephalus

title: Pulmonary Embolism: Acute Management and Rehabilitation
content:
### Blood Clot in Pulmonary Vasculature
Potentially life-threatening condition requiring careful monitoring.

### PT Considerations
*   **Bed Rest Controversy**: Early mobilization when stable
*   **Anticoagulation**: Fall prevention essential
*   **Oxygen**: Maintain adequate saturation
*   **Progressive Activity**: Monitor vital signs and symptoms
*   **DVT Prevention**: Continue prophylaxis

title: Femoroacetabular Impingement (FAI)
content:
### Hip Joint Abnormality
Cam (femoral) or pincer (acetabular) morphology causing impingement.

### Conservative Management
*   **Activity Modification**: Avoid deep flexion and rotation
*   **Strengthening**: Hip abductors and rotators
*   **ROM**: Within pain-free range
*   **Core Stability**: Lumbopelvic control
*   **Surgical Indication**: Failed conservative treatment with mechanical symptoms

title: Charcot-Marie-Tooth Disease
content:
### Hereditary Motor and Sensory Neuropathy
Progressive weakness and sensory loss in distal extremities.

### Management Approach
*   **Orthoses**: AFOs for foot drop and stability
*   **Strengthening**: Maintain function without overwork
*   **Fall Prevention**: Address balance deficits
*   **Energy Conservation**: Fatigue management
*   **Monitoring**: Track progression and adjust interventions

title: Ankylosing Spondylitis Exercise Program
content:
### Inflammatory Spondyloarthropathy
Affects spine and sacroiliac joints, risk of ankylosis.

### Exercise Prescription
*   **Posture Training**: Prevent kyphotic deformity
*   **Spinal Extension**: Maintain/improve mobility
*   **Breathing Exercises**: Chest expansion
*   **Aerobic Exercise**: Swimming ideal
*   **NSAIDs**: Before exercise may reduce pain and stiffness

title: Hypertension: Exercise Prescription
content:
### Elevated Blood Pressure Management
Exercise is first-line treatment for pre-hypertension and Stage 1 HTN.

### Exercise Guidelines
*   **Aerobic**: 150 min/week moderate or 75 min vigorous
*   **Resistance**: 2-3 days/week, avoid Valsalva
*   **Precautions**: Severe uncontrolled HTN (>180/110)
*   **Expected Reduction**: 5-7 mmHg in systolic BP
*   **Monitoring**: Check BP before and after sessions

title: Myasthenia Gravis: Fatigue Management
content:
### Autoimmune Neuromuscular Junction Disorder
Fluctuating muscle weakness worsening with activity.

### PT Interventions
*   **Energy Conservation**: Frequent rest breaks
*   **Timing**: Exercise when medication peak (1-2 hours post-dose)
*   **Avoid Overwork**: Can worsen symptoms
*   **Respiratory Monitoring**: Risk of myasthenic crisis
*   **Assistive Devices**: Compensate for weakness

title: Peripheral Neuropathy: Fall Prevention
content:
### Sensory and Motor Nerve Damage
Increased fall risk due to impaired proprioception and strength.

### Intervention Strategies
*   **Sensory Substitution**: Visual and vestibular compensation
*   **Balance Training**: Challenging but safe progressions
*   **Footwear**: Proper fit, non-slip soles
*   **Home Safety**: Remove hazards, adequate lighting
*   **Assistive Devices**: Consider for severe impairment

title: Shoulder Impingement Syndrome
content:
### Subacromial Pain
Compression of rotator cuff tendons and bursa under acromion.

### Treatment Approach
*   **Activity Modification**: Avoid overhead activities initially
*   **Scapular Stabilization**: Address scapular dyskinesis
*   **Rotator Cuff Strengthening**: Progressive resistance
*   **Manual Therapy**: Joint mobilization and soft tissue
*   **Postural Correction**: Thoracic extension, reduce forward head

title: Traumatic Amputation: Lower Limb Prosthetic Training
content:
### Post-Amputation Rehabilitation
Goal is independent ambulation with prosthesis.

### Rehabilitation Phases
*   **Pre-Prosthetic**: Residual limb care, ROM, strengthening
*   **Prosthetic Training**: Donning/doffing, weight shifting, gait
*   **Advanced Training**: Stairs, uneven terrain, running
*   **Challenges**: Phantom pain, skin breakdown, energy cost

title: Cerebral Aneurysm: Post-Surgical Rehabilitation
content:
### Intracranial Aneurysm Repair
Surgical or endovascular repair followed by rehabilitation.

### PT Considerations
*   **Precautions**: Blood pressure management, Valsalva avoidance
*   **Cognitive Deficits**: Common, require adaptive approaches
*   **Early Mobilization**: Once medically stable
*   **Vasospasm Risk**: Peak 7-10 days post-bleed
*   **Gradual Progression**: Monitor for neurological changes

title: Developmental Dysplasia of the Hip (DDH)
content:
### Hip Joint Abnormality in Infants
Range from mild instability to complete dislocation.

### Treatment by Age and Severity
*   **<6 months**: Pavlik harness
*   **6-18 months**: Closed or open reduction, spica cast
*   **>18 months**: Often requires open reduction
*   **PT Role**: Monitor development, address ROM after immobilization

title: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS)
content:
### Severe Lung Injury
Diffuse alveolar damage requiring mechanical ventilation.

### PT Interventions
*   **Prone Positioning**: Improves oxygenation in severe cases
*   **Early Mobilization**: When hemodynamically stable
*   **Secretion Management**: Minimize if possible due to vulnerability
*   **Progressive Activity**: Very gradual due to deconditioning
*   **Long-Term**: ICU-acquired weakness common

title: Piriformis Syndrome
content:
### Sciatic Nerve Compression
Piriformis muscle compresses sciatic nerve causing buttock pain.

### Treatment Approach
*   **Stretching**: Piriformis and hip rotators
*   **Strengthening**: Hip stabilizers, address muscle imbalances
*   **Manual Therapy**: Soft tissue release, neural mobilization
*   **Activity Modification**: Avoid prolonged sitting
*   **Diagnosis Challenge**: Rule out lumbar spine pathology

title: Chronic Fatigue Syndrome/ME: Exercise Therapy
content:
### Post-Exertional Malaise
Exercise prescription requires careful grading to avoid symptom exacerbation.

### Modified Exercise Approach
*   **Pacing**: Stay within energy envelope
*   **Graded Activity**: Very gradual increases
*   **Monitoring**: Track symptoms and adjust accordingly
*   **Energy Conservation**: Essential component
*   **Controversy**: Debate over graded exercise therapy effectiveness

title: Thoracic Outlet Syndrome
content:
### Neurovascular Compression
Compression of brachial plexus and/or subclavian vessels.

### Conservative Management
*   **Postural Correction**: Reduce forward head and rounded shoulders
*   **Stretching**: Scalenes, pectoralis minor
*   **Strengthening**: Scapular stabilizers
*   **Neural Mobilization**: Careful brachial plexus glides
*   **Activity Modification**: Avoid overhead and provocative positions

title: Postpartum Diastasis Recti
content:
### Abdominal Muscle Separation
Separation of rectus abdominis muscles during pregnancy.

### Rehabilitation Approach
*   **Assessment**: Measure inter-recti distance and tension
*   **Core Activation**: Transverse abdominis focus
*   **Avoid**: Traditional crunches, excessive intra-abdominal pressure
*   **Functional Training**: Integrate core in daily activities
*   **Timeline**: May improve up to 8-12 months postpartum

title: Myofascial Pain Syndrome: Trigger Point Treatment
content:
### Chronic Pain Condition
Taut muscle bands with hypersensitive trigger points.

### Treatment Modalities
*   **Manual Therapy**: Ischemic compression, spray and stretch
*   **Dry Needling**: If trained and in scope
*   **Stretching**: Post-treatment to maintain length
*   **Postural Correction**: Address perpetuating factors
*   **Patient Education**: Avoid aggravating activities

title: Pediatric Obesity: Exercise Programming
content:
### Childhood Overweight and Obesity
Multifactorial problem requiring family-centered approach.

### Exercise Recommendations
*   **Frequency**: 60 minutes daily moderate-vigorous activity
*   **Type**: Fun, varied activities to promote adherence
*   **Family Involvement**: Whole family lifestyle changes
*   **Reduce Sedentary**: Limit screen time to <2 hours/day
*   **Psychosocial**: Address self-esteem and bullying

title: Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome: Hypermobility Type
content:
### Connective Tissue Disorder
Generalized joint hypermobility with chronic pain and instability.

### Management Strategy
*   **Strengthening**: Focus on joint stability, avoid overstretching
*   **Proprioception**: Enhance joint position sense
*   **Joint Protection**: Avoid hyperextension
*   **Energy Conservation**: Manage fatigue
*   **Pain Management**: Multidisciplinary approach

title: Atrial Fibrillation: Exercise Prescription
content:
### Irregular Heart Rhythm
Exercise safe for most patients with controlled AFib.

### Exercise Guidelines
*   **Rate Control**: Ensure ventricular rate controlled
*   **Anticoagulation**: Fall risk consideration
*   **Monitoring**: May have irregular pulse, use RPE
*   **Intensity**: Moderate generally safe
*   **Contraindications**: Uncontrolled rate, symptomatic

title: IT Band Syndrome (Iliotibial Band Friction Syndrome)
content:
### Lateral Knee Pain in Runners
Overuse injury from IT band friction over lateral femoral condyle.

### Treatment Protocol
*   **Activity Modification**: Reduce mileage, avoid hills
*   **Stretching**: IT band, TFL, hip flexors
*   **Strengthening**: Hip abductors, particularly gluteus medius
*   **Foam Rolling**: IT band and TFL
*   **Running Gait**: Address excessive hip adduction

title: Hemophilia: Exercise and Bleeding Precautions
content:
### Clotting Factor Deficiency
Increased bleeding risk, especially into joints (hemarthrosis).

### Exercise Recommendations
*   **Safe Activities**: Swimming, cycling, walking
*   **Avoid**: Contact sports, high-impact activities
*   **Prophylaxis**: Factor replacement before exercise if severe
*   **Joint Protection**: Strengthen muscles to protect joints
*   **Early Treatment**: RICE for any bleeds

title: Post-Concussion Syndrome Management
content:
### Persistent Post-Concussive Symptoms
Symptoms lasting beyond typical recovery period (>2 weeks).

### Rehabilitation Approach
*   **Gradual Return**: Buffalo Concussion Treadmill Test protocol
*   **Vestibular Rehabilitation**: If dizziness/balance issues
*   **Cervical Treatment**: Often co-existing neck injury
*   **Visual Therapy**: For vision-related symptoms
*   **Exertion Management**: Sub-symptom threshold activity

questionText:
A patient following a stroke demonstrates a significant knee hyperextension (genu recurvatum) during the midstance phase of gait. You note significant weakness in the quadriceps muscle group (3/5 MMT) and moderate spasticity in the plantarflexors.

Which orthotic adjustment or intervention is MOST likely to improve this specific gait deviation?

options.A: Setting the AFO in 5 degrees of plantarflexion
options.B: Applying a heel lift to the contralateral limb
options.C: Using a knee cage to lock the joint
options.D: Ankle-foot orthosis (AFO) set in 5 degrees of dorsiflexion

explanation: Knee hyperextension in stance is often caused by either weak quadriceps (unable to control knee flexion) or tight/spastic plantarflexors (pulling the tibia back). By setting an AFO in a few degrees of dorsiflexion, you create a flexion moment at the knee during stance, which physically prevents the 'snapping back' into hyperextension. Setting it in plantarflexion would actually worsen the hyperextension. A heel lift on the other side does not address the biomechanical fault of the affected limb.
